macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Shlakot (Vegetable Soup) — Prooftext from Deuteronomy and the Scope of Non-Jewish Cooking Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context: the daf location and the meaning of shlakot
Tosafot's question on the source for the shlakot prohibition and the reasoning of chatnut (intermarriage concern)
The prooftext from Deuteronomy 2:27 — Moshe's request for food and water to the King of Amorites
The principle of kamayim (like water) — food that does not change from its original nature
Discussion of roasted grains and ground grains — where the line of change is drawn
The heating/cooking element in the kamayim principle — foods not changed by fire
Derabbanan status and asmachta — the prohibition is rabbinic with a Biblical allusion
Rashi's reasoning: fear of becoming accustomed and eating forbidden foods; comparison with Tosafot's chatnut emphasis

macro_segments.title: The Ra'avad's Lenient Opinion and Rejection — Non-Jewish Cooking in a Jew's Home versus the Non-Jew's Home

micro_segments.title: The Ra'avad's position: permitted when non-Jew cooks in the Jew's home; no concern for intermarriage or forbidden foods
Rejection by Rabbeinu Tam and Tosafot — non-Jew might be careless even in Jew's home or bring outside ingredients
Modern implications: Non-Jewish domestic workers and catering — practical halakhic questions

macro_segments.title: The Categories of Permitted Foods — Raw, Ben Drosai, and Non-Cooking Processes

micro_segments.title: Rav Shmuel bar Yitzchak's teaching: foods eaten raw (chi) have no issue of non-Jewish cooking
Pumbedita's alternative criterion: foods fit for royal tables and eaten raw
Examples of permitted foods: small salted fish, mushrooms, and daisa (porridge); the question of salting and preservation
Rav Yosef's ruling on fried fish and the forbidden dish ksada d'harsana — fish with flour and oil, possibly containing wine-based vinegar
The question of ikar (main ingredient) in mixed dishes — is it the kosher fish or the flour?
Tosafot's discussion of honey and cheese: raw, eaten with bread, fit for royal tables — whether they require a final Jewish cooking action

macro_segments.title: Intent and Agency in Cooking — Non-Jewish and Jewish Participation in the Cooking Process

micro_segments.title: Case: Non-Jew singeing an animal's head to remove hair — intent matters; ear is permitted despite full cooking
Case: Non-Jew placing a peg in an oven versus Jew placing gourd in oven — who intended to cook and whether it matters
Tosafot's requirement: intent of cooking (kavanah) is needed, not merely incidental heating
Case: Jew places meat on coals, non-Jew turns it — permitted because Jew initiated and non-Jew merely maintains
Discussion of hastening vs. causing cooking — does non-Jew's intervention that speeds up inevitable cooking constitute bishul?
Rabbi Yochanan's rule: either Jew initiates or non-Jew initiates, but not both non-Jewish from start to finish (techilo v'gemaro)
Ravina's cases: various combinations of non-Jew and Jew lighting oven and baking; Jew stoking fire (chituy) afterward is sufficient

macro_segments.title: Specific Foods — Salted Fish, Roasted Egg, and Judicial Rulings on Permissibility

micro_segments.title: Dispute: salted fish (dag maliach) — Chizkiyah permits, Rabbi Yochanan forbids
Dispute: roasted egg (beitza tzluyah) — Bar Kappara permits, Rabbi Yochanan forbids
Story: Rabbi Chiya Parva in the house of Reish Galuta — neither opinion prevails over two; Rav Zevid enforces Rabbi Yochanan's view and faces punishment
Baraita: capers, leeks, matlya, hot water, roasted grains are permitted; egg with oil was reconsidered by Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi and his court and permitted

macro_segments.title: The Matlya and Shiata — A Sprouting Seed Dish and Magical or Idolatrous Connotations

micro_segments.title: Definition of matlya as pashla or shiata — unclear term requiring clarification
Detailed process of shiata: soaking parsley, flax, and fenugreek seeds in lukewarm water until sprouting; placing in red clay pot
Use of shiata: cooling the body after bathhouse; emerging from bathhouse to find seeds sprouted and blossomed
Rav Ashi's skepticism: the story is baseless or involves magical words (milin), not natural growth

macro_segments.title: Non-Jewish Beverages and Cooked Foods — Boiled Dates, Kusfan Wine, and Sweet versus Bitter

micro_segments.title: Kusfan: alcoholic beverage made from date soakings; forbidden in large vessel (porous opening), permitted in small vessel (opening too small for sparrow)
Definition of small vessel: head of sparrow cannot enter; concern about added ingredients or contamination
Conflicting baraita: size difference does not determine permissibility; resolved via distinction between one who says noten taam lifgam is assur and one who says mutar

macro_segments.title: Noten Taam Lifgam — Residual Flavoring in Vessels and the Permissibility of Reused Cookware

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's ruling: non-Jewish oil (mishcha) cooked by Arameans is forbidden
Rav Safra's inquiry: what is the concern — mixed ingredients, forbidden cooking, or residual flavor (noten taam lifgam)?
Analysis: if noten taam lifgam, it is permitted because it imparts undesirable flavor that would be noticeable or not change the food
Tosafot on double doubt (sfek sfeka): uncertainty about when vessel was used AND uncertainty about what substance was used makes it permitted

macro_segments.title: Non-Jewish Beverages and Cooked Foods — Boiled Dates, Kusfan Wine, and Sweet versus Bitter (continued)

micro_segments.title: Query regarding Aramean boiled dates: sweet dates are permitted (no additives), bitter dates are forbidden (cooked with vinegar from wine), middle category uncertain
Rabbi Assi's student reports: Rabbi forbade middle category; thus the halakha follows leniency of Levi on sweet and stringency on bitter

macro_segments.title: The Shtita Dish — Lentil or Wheat Flour Preparation with Vinegar and Biblical Allusion

micro_segments.title: Definition of shtita: dish made from parched grain flour or lentil flour; lentils are sweet and vinegar may be added due to sweetness
Concern with vinegar: comes from wine; wine might be yayin nesech (idolatrous wine); therefore shtita made with vinegar is problematic
Further discussion to continue on Daf 67 regarding permissibility of shtita and related dishes

macro_segments.title: Closing: Visual Identification of the Caper Plant and Its Parts — Botanical Context for Talmudic Foods

micro_segments.title: The tzalaf (caper) plant from Jerusalem: caper buds (kafrisin), berries (evyonot), leaves all from same plant; each part with distinct berakhot

topical_tags.term: shlakot (vegetable soup — water and food mixture)
bishulei akum / bishulei ovdei kochavim (non-Jewish cooking prohibition)
chatnut (intermarriage concern as reason for prohibition)
kamayim (like water — unchanged food principle)
mum / nigmur / chitin vetachanan (change through grinding vs. roasting)
derabbanan (rabbinic enactment with asmachta — Biblical allusion only)
regilut (becoming accustomed — Rashi's concern)
davar tamei / forbidden foods mixed in (concern)
Ra'avad (Ravad) — lenient opinion on cooking in Jew's home
Rabbeinu Tam — rejection of Ra'avad
chi / chayim (raw, eaten uncooked)
ben drosai (minimally cooked, like robber's quick meal — one-third cooked threshold)
shulchan melachim (royal table foods, eaten raw)
dag maliach (salted fish — non-cooking process)
daisa (porridge, cream of wheat — cooked minimally)
kalei habishul (quick-cooking foods)
ksada d'harsana (fish dish with flour, oil, vinegar — forbidden complex dish)
ikar (main ingredient in mixed dish — determines status)
charach / singeing (removing hair from animal head — non-cooking intent)
techilo v'gemaro (start and finish by non-Jew — forbidden; mixed participation permitted)
kavanah / intent in cooking
melechet machshevet (intentional craft-work — philosophical underpinning)
chi (raw food) vs. bashil (cooked food) distinction
hastening cooking (does non-Jew's intervention that speeds inevitable cooking constitute bishul?)
chituy (stoking/stirring of fire — Shabbat terminology, used for Jewish participation)
beitza tzluyah (roasted egg — dispute on permissibility)
Chizkiyah (lenient authority on salted fish and roasted egg)
Bar Kappara (lenient authority on roasted egg)
Rabbi Yochanan (stringent on salted fish and roasted egg; key authority throughout daf)
Rav Dimi (reporter of unified position on fish and egg)
Reish Galuta (head of diaspora community, consulted on egg ruling)
Rav Zevid (enforcer of stringent halakha, faces consequences)
Abaye (authority whose opinion on halakha is cited by Rav Zevid)
democracy of opinions (two vs. one — not always used in Talmud)
kafrisin (capers — food permitted when cooked by non-Jew)
kaflotot (leeks — food permitted when cooked by non-Jew)
matlya / pashla / shiata (sprouting seed dish — unclear term)
kaliyot (roasted grains — permitted)
Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi / Nasi (reconsidered egg with oil — permitted)
Rabbah bar bar Chana (transmitter of Rabbi Yochanan's teachings)
Rav Shmuel bar Yitzchak (amar Rav: chi/raw food criterion)
Rav Assi (amar Rabbi Yochanan: royal table criterion; also questioned on boiled dates)
Rav Yosef (ruling on fried fish and ksada d'harsana)
Rav Beruna (case of burning meadow and grasshoppers)
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak (kal vachomer logic on participation)
Ravina (cases of bread and oven, Jew's fire participation)
Rav Ashi (skeptical of shiata sprouting story)
Rav Sheshet (non-Jewish oil forbidden)
Rav Safra (inquiry into reasons for oil restriction)
Rav Zevid (enforcer of stringency, dies after drinking)
Rabbi Chanina (mentioned regarding shiata story)
Rabbi Yannai (definition of small vessel — bird cannot enter)
Rav Hanan bar Ami (amar Rav Adda amar Rabbi Yochanan: singeing hair)
yayin nesech (wine from idolatrous practices — concern with vinegar-based dishes)
noten taam lifgam (residual flavor that detracts — permitted because noticeable/non-improving)
sfek sfeka (double doubt — permits where single doubt might forbid)
kusfan (date-based fermented beverage; forbidden in large vessel, permitted in small)
yora gedolah / yora ketanah (large vs. small vessel for fermented drinks)
ahini shlakei d'Aramaye (boiled dates from Arameans)
sweet vs. bitter dates (permitted vs. forbidden based on additives)
shtita (lentil or wheat flour dish — concerns with vinegar/wine)
hashgacha (supervision/certification — modern context)
cholent (slow-cooked Shabbat dish — example of concern with non-Jew adding ingredients)
bishul on Shabbat (cooking prohibitions on Shabbat — related halachot)
pesach / Passover context (mentioned regarding oven practices)
King David (Biblical allusion to come — regarding shtita)
Tosafot (medieval French-German commentators — positions on intent and participation)
Rashi (medieval French commentator — concerns about habituation)
Rambam (Maimonides — lenient on peg-throwing into fire)
Shulchan Aruch (code of Jewish law — positions differ on Ashkenazi vs. Sefardi practice)
Ashkenazi vs. Sefardi (different practices on Jewish intervention in non-Jewish cooking)
Rosh (Asher ben Yechiel — forbids peg practice but lenient overall)
tzalaf / caper plant botanical reference
